findings.title: VAPID private key committed to git in 4 locations
findings.description: The same VAPID private key (dVpMJM8ZFeQj...) is committed in .env.development (git-tracked), hardcoded as fallback in server config, used as-is in railway.env.example, and in docker-compose. Anyone with repo read access can impersonate the push notification sender.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Clone the repo. 2. Read VAPID private key from any of the 4 locations. 3. Use web-push library to send push notifications impersonating the app. 4. Send phishing notifications to all subscribed users.
findings.impact: Attacker can send push notifications to all subscribed users, enabling phishing, social engineering, or directing users to malicious URLs.
findings.recommendation: 1. Generate new VAPID keys. 2. Set them via environment variables only. 3. Remove hardcoded fallback from config/index.js. 4. Add .env.development to .gitignore. 5. Use placeholder values in example files. 6. Scrub history with git filter-repo.

findings.title: No rate limiting on login endpoint enables brute-force attacks
findings.description: The POST /api/auth/login endpoint has no rate limiting, no account lockout after failed attempts, and no CAPTCHA. Combined with the default admin password (admin123) and 6-char minimum, brute-force is trivially feasible.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Target demo@dashboard.com or superadmin@dashboard.com. 2. Run automated password attempts at full speed. 3. With 6-char minimum and no lockout, dictionary attacks succeed in minutes. 4. Gain admin or super-admin access.
findings.impact: Full account takeover. Super-admin access allows managing all users, companies, and data.
findings.recommendation: Add express-rate-limit: 5 attempts per 15 minutes per IP on /api/auth/login. Add account lockout after 10 failed attempts. Require password change on first login for seeded accounts.

findings.title: Unauthenticated debug endpoint exposes server internals
findings.description: GET /api/payments/debug/timezone has no requireAuth middleware. Exposes server timezone, Intl configuration, date processing logic, and server time. Mounted on the payments router which is public by default for this route.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Send GET /api/payments/debug/timezone?timezone=UTC. 2. Receive server time, timezone config, date processing behavior. 3. Use server time leak to time attacks or understand session expiry windows.
findings.impact: Information disclosure. Server internals exposed to unauthenticated users.
findings.recommendation: Add requireAuth middleware to the debug/timezone route, or remove it entirely from production builds.

findings.title: Default admin credentials in auto-run migrations
findings.description: Migrations insert demo@dashboard.com/admin123 and superadmin@dashboard.com/superadmin123. Comments reveal plaintext passwords. Migrations auto-run on startup via ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING, so these persist if never changed.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Discover the app (public Railway URL). 2. Try demo@dashboard.com / admin123. 3. Gain admin access. 4. Try superadmin@dashboard.com / superadmin123. 5. Gain super-admin access to all companies.
findings.impact: Full system compromise via known default credentials.
findings.recommendation: 1. Remove plaintext password comments from migration files. 2. Force password change on first login for seeded accounts. 3. Use environment-based initial credentials for production.

findings.title: Wildcard CORS allows any origin to make authenticated requests
findings.description: app.use(cors()) with no configuration sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *. Any website can make cross-origin requests to the API. Since auth is via x-session-id header (not cookies), CORS alone doesn't gate access, but it removes the browser's cross-origin defense layer entirely.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker hosts malicious page. 2. Page makes fetch requests to the API with a stolen session token. 3. No origin restriction prevents the request. 4. Combined with XSS or session theft, enables full remote exploitation.
findings.impact: Removes browser cross-origin protections. Amplifies impact of any session theft or XSS.
findings.recommendation: Configure CORS with explicit allowed origins: cors({ origin: ['https://your-domain.com'], credentials: true }).

findings.title: Admin role change not scoped to company
findings.description: PUT /users/:userId/role allows admin-role users to change roles of ANY user in the system. The endpoint blocks admins from setting super-admin role, but does not verify the target user belongs to the admin's company. An admin from Company A can demote admins in Company B to employee.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Log in as admin of Company A. 2. Enumerate user IDs. 3. PUT /api/admin/users/{companyB-userId}/role with {role: 'employee'}. 4. Demote Company B's admin to employee, disrupting their operations.
findings.impact: Cross-tenant privilege manipulation. Admins can affect users outside their company.
findings.recommendation: Add company scope check for admin role (same pattern as the hourly-rate and status endpoints).

findings.title: Time entry edit not scoped to company
findings.description: PUT /payroll/admin/entries/:id checks role but does NOT verify the time entry belongs to the admin's company. Any admin can edit any time entry in the system by guessing/enumerating IDs.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Log in as admin. 2. Enumerate time entry IDs. 3. PUT /api/payroll/admin/entries/{id} with modified clock_in_at/clock_out_at/amount. 4. Modify payroll data for employees in other companies.
findings.impact: Cross-tenant data manipulation. Financial impact via payroll tampering.
findings.recommendation: Join time_entries to users to verify the entry's user belongs to the admin's company before allowing updates.

findings.title: Production Dockerfile runs as root
findings.description: The production Dockerfile has no USER directive. The Node.js application runs as root inside the container.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Exploit any RCE vulnerability in the app or a dependency. 2. Gain root access inside the container. 3. With root, escape container isolation or access mounted volumes/secrets.
findings.impact: Container escape risk. Root access amplifies any code execution vulnerability.
findings.recommendation: Add to Dockerfile: RUN addgroup -g 1001 -S nodejs && adduser -S nextjs -u 1001 && USER nextjs

findings.title: Stack traces leaked in error responses
findings.description: The /subscribe and /test-capabilities endpoints return error.stack in JSON responses. This exposes internal file paths, code structure, and dependency versions to clients.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Trigger an error in /api/notifications/subscribe (e.g., malformed subscription data). 2. Read the stack trace from the response. 3. Map internal file structure and dependency versions. 4. Use info to target specific known vulnerabilities.
findings.impact: Information disclosure. Internal code paths and dependency versions exposed.
findings.recommendation: Never return error.stack in production responses. Log it server-side only. Return generic error messages to clients.

findings.title: axios has 5 known vulnerabilities including SSRF
findings.description: axios ^1.6.2 has critical SSRF via header injection and cloud metadata exfiltration vulnerabilities. This is a direct production dependency used for all OlaClick API calls. No automated fix available.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. If user-controlled data flows into HTTP headers via axios (e.g., forwarded headers), craft a payload that injects additional headers. 2. Redirect requests to cloud metadata endpoint (169.254.169.254). 3. Exfiltrate cloud credentials.
findings.impact: Potential SSRF and credential exfiltration in cloud environments.
findings.recommendation: Upgrade axios to the latest version. Verify no user-controlled data flows into axios request headers.

findings.title: node-forge (via web-push) has 7 vulnerabilities including signature forgery
findings.description: web-push depends on node-forge which has critical RSA-PKCS and Ed25519 signature forgery, certificate chain bypass, and DoS vulnerabilities. Fix available via npm audit fix.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Forge a certificate or signature that node-forge incorrectly validates. 2. Bypass TLS certificate validation in push notification flows.
findings.impact: Potential certificate/signature forgery in push notification infrastructure.
findings.recommendation: Run npm audit fix to update node-forge. If web-push doesn't update its dependency, consider patching via overrides.

findings.title: .env.development tracked by git
findings.description: .gitignore excludes .env but NOT .env.development. The file contains VAPID keys and is committed to the repo.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Any collaborator or anyone with repo access reads .env.development. 2. Obtains VAPID keys and DB configuration.
findings.impact: Secret exposure to anyone with repository access.
findings.recommendation: Add .env.* (except .env.example) to .gitignore. Remove .env.development from git tracking: git rm --cached .env.development

findings.title: Default DB password fallback in code
findings.description: If DB_PASSWORD env var is unset, the code silently falls back to password: 'postgres'. Combined with default user 'postgres', this could enable unauthorized DB access if deployed with misconfigured environment.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Deploy to new environment without setting DB_PASSWORD. 2. App silently connects with postgres/postgres. 3. If DB is accessible, anyone can also connect with these credentials.
findings.impact: Unauthorized database access on misconfigured deployments.
findings.recommendation: Throw an error if DB_PASSWORD is not set in production. Remove default fallbacks for all DB connection params in production mode.

findings.title: Stored XSS via v-html with user-derived content
findings.description: v-html renders a tooltip containing userName(entry.user_id) and other entry data. User names come from the database without sanitization. An admin who creates a user with a malicious name (e.g., ) triggers XSS in any admin viewing the payroll page.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Admin creates user with name: . 2. Another admin opens the payroll calendar. 3. Hovers over the time entry for that user. 4. Tooltip renders the name via v-html, executing the script. 5. Attacker steals session token from localStorage.
findings.impact: Session hijacking. Any admin viewing payroll can have their session stolen.
findings.recommendation: Replace v-html with a structured Vue template using {{ }} text interpolation (which auto-escapes). Or use DOMPurify to sanitize the content.

findings.title: SSL certificate validation disabled for production DB connection
findings.description: Production database connection uses ssl: { rejectUnauthorized: false }. This disables TLS certificate verification, making the connection vulnerable to MITM attacks.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Position on the network path between app and database. 2. Present a self-signed certificate. 3. Intercept all DB traffic including credentials, queries, and responses.
findings.impact: Database credentials and data vulnerable to MITM on the network path.
findings.recommendation: Set rejectUnauthorized: true and provide the correct CA certificate for your database provider. Railway provides a CA cert that should be configured.

findings.title: Sessions not invalidated on password change
findings.description: When a user changes their password, existing sessions are not invalidated. If an attacker has a stolen session token, changing the password does not revoke their access.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker steals session token (via XSS, network sniffing, etc). 2. Victim discovers the breach and changes their password. 3. Attacker's session token remains valid for up to 24 hours.
findings.impact: Compromised sessions persist even after password change.
findings.recommendation: After password change, invalidate all sessions for that user except the current one: UPDATE user_sessions SET is_active = FALSE WHERE user_id = $1 AND session_token != $2
